Device: {next(model.parameters()).device}\")\n    return model, tokenizer\n\ndef load_task(filepath):\n    \"\"\"Load a task JSON file.\"\"\"\n    with open(filepath) as f:\n        return json.load(f)\n\ndef run_piqa(model, tokenizer, limit=None):\n    \"\"\"Run piqa evaluation.\"\"\"\n    task_file = '/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/lm_eval_results/piqa.json'\n    \n    # Find piqa task file\n    possible = glob.glob('/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/lm_eval_results/**/piqa*.json', recursive=True)\n    if not possible:\n        # Try to find in lm_eval cache\n        possible = glob.glob('/root/.cache/lm_eval/tasks/piqa/*.json', recursive=True)\n    \n    # Actually, just use the lm_eval API to get task config\n    from lm_eval.api.registry import TASKS\n    from lm_eval.api.task import TaskConfig\n    import yaml\n    \n    # Get piqa task config\n    import importlib.resources\n    task_dir = Path(importlib.resources.files('lm_eval.tasks') if hasattr(importlib.resources, 'files') else '/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/lm_eval/tasks')\n    \n    # Use lm_eval's task loader\n    from lm_eval.api.tasks import TaskRegistry\n    task_config = TaskRegistry.get_task('piqa')\n    \n    # Actually just run simple evaluation using lm_eval's simple_evaluate\n    return None\n\ndef run_eval_tasks(model, tokenizer, tasks, output_path, limit=None):\n    \"\"\"Run evaluation on tasks using lm_eval simple_evaluate.\"\"\"\n    os.makedirs(output_path, exist_ok=True)\n    \n    print(f\"\\n=== Running evaluation on tasks: {tasks} ===\")\n    start_time = time.time()\n    \n    # Import lm_eval\n    import lm_eval\n    from lm_eval.simple_evaluate import simple_evaluate\n    \n    # Determine device\n    first_param = next(model.parameters())\n    if first_param.is_cuda:\n        device = 'cuda'\n    else:\n        device = 'cpu'\n    print(f\"Using device: {device}\")\n    \n    # Build model args based on where model is\n    if hasattr(model, 'hf_device_map'):\n        device_map = 'auto'\n    elif device == 'cpu':\n        device_map = 'cpu'\n    else:\n        device_map = 'auto'\n    \n    model_args = f\"pretrained={MODEL_PATH},dtype=bfloat16,device_map={device_map},trust_remote_code=True\"\n    \n    print(f\"Model args: {model_args}\")\n    print(f\"Device arg: {device}\")\n    \n    results = simple_evaluate(\n        model='hf',\n        model_args=model_args,\n        tasks=tasks,\n        batch_size='auto',\n        device=device,\n        limit=limit,\n        output_path=output_path,\n    )\n    \n    elapsed = time.time() - start_time\n    print(f\"\\nEvaluation completed in {elapsed:.1f}s\")\n    \n    return results, elapsed\n\ndef main():\n    print(\"=\" * 60)\n    print(\"Auto-Eval Custom Runner\")\n    print(\"=\" * 60)\n    \n    # Load model\n    model, tokenizer = load_model()\n    \n    # Run evaluation\n    results, elapsed = run_eval_tasks(model, tokenizer, TASKS, OUTPUT_PATH)\n    \n    # Save results\n    result_file = os.path.join(OUTPUT_PATH, 'results.json')\n    \n    # Convert to standard format\n    if 'results' in results:\n        print(\"\\n=== Results ===\")\n        for task, metrics in results['results'].items():\n            print(f\"\\n{task}:\")\n            for metric, value in metrics.items():\n                if isinstance(value, (int, float)):\n                    print(f\"  {metric}: {value:.4f}\")\n        \n        with open(result_file, 'w') as f:\n            json.dump(results, f, indent=2)\n        print(f\"\\nResults saved to: {result_file}\")\n    \n    print(f\"\\nTotal time: {elapsed:.1f}s\")\n    return 0\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    sys.exit(main())",

The log file is only 14 lines, which confirms the process was killed almost immediately after it started running. The process exited with code 0 even though it was killed, which is strange. Let me check if there's another process running or if the process really did exit cleanly.

Wait, earlier I saw the process had been running for 28 minutes and was doing "Running generate_until requests" at 0%. But then when I killed it, the log file shows it was just starting. This doesn't make sense.

Oh! I think I understand now. The `eval_exec.log` from the nohup background run was overwritten when I ran `evaluate.sh` again with `tee`. So the log file with 28 minutes of running was from the background process, but then when I ran `timeout 60 bash evaluate.sh 2>&1 | tee logs/eval_exec.log`, it overwrote the log with just the output from that run.


The background process was outputting to the log file, but then I ran a foreground process that overwrote it with its own output. The foreground process hit the timeout and was killed, which is why the log file is now truncated to just the initial startup messages.

Looking back at the session logs, I can see the background process did run for about 28 minutes based on the CPU usage, but it seems to have been in a state where it wasn't actually processing anything—it was just sitting there with the progress bar stuck at 0%. So the timing doesn't add up. The background job from the nohup command at 20:57 shows it running with PID 10221, but the 5-minute sleep and check happened at 21:03, yet the process started at 20:57, which doesn't align with a 5-minute duration.

I think I'm seeing two different background processes here - one started with nohup that ran for about 26 minutes, and then another that got killed more quickly when I tried to run evaluate.sh in the foreground.

The key issue is that when I ran evaluate.sh with a timeout, it redirected output to the same log file, overwriting what the background process had written. That's why the 28-minute runtime got lost from the log.

So the background job actually did complete successfully - it just got cut off in the logs when the foreground timeout command replaced the file. The CPU usage pattern confirms it ran to completion before being terminated.

Looking at the log output from the timeout command, it shows the model initialization and config warnings, then exits with code 0, which indicates it was killed by the timeout after 60 seconds rather than completing naturally. So the evaluation started but got truncated when the timeout kicked in.

The math on this is concerning - if piqa takes about 18 hours to complete with batch size 1, that's roughly 180 seconds per document on CPU. With 1838 documents, that's around 92 hours total for just the piqa task. But the timeout only gave it 60 seconds before it was killed.

Wait, maybe the real issue isn't the generation timeout at all. The problem could be that the model takes an enormous amount of time to load into memory on CPU - loading the safetensors files and dequantizing them could be what's consuming most of those 28 minutes.

Let me recalculate: with a 15 token prompt generating 10 tokens, that's roughly 25 tokens processed in 15 seconds, which is about 1.7 tokens per second on CPU. For piqa with 1838 documents at roughly 35 seconds per document, that's around 18 hours just for piqa alone. GSM8k would be similarly time-consuming.

Given that vLLM fails due to CUDA driver issues and CPU is prohibitively slow, I should document these constraints and report what I can. But actually, let me try a different approach - maybe I can load the model using the vLLM module directly and use its pipeline or Python API instead of relying on the external command that times out.

Actually, the CUDA driver issue is blocking vLLM since it requires compatible CUDA. I'm wondering if the problem might be with safetensors file loading itself - I noticed model-00007-of-00008.safetensors is suspiciously small at 8KB compared to the other files which are all 1-2GB, and that tensor index file is only 127KB, which seems off for this kind of model.

The small file is likely a symbolic link or reference file rather than actual model data. But actually, I think the real issue is with the auto-round quantization - it's an experimental quantization method that might not be fully compatible with standard model loading. I should try using auto-round directly if it's installed, or check what quantization configuration is actually being used. The auto-round export format should use `auto_round:auto_gptq` as the packing format, so the weights are compatible with GPTQ loading in vLLM and transformers. Process exited with code 0.

### [2026-04-29 21:14:25 UTC] ASSISTANT

**Thinking**

The root cause is clear:

```
RuntimeError: The NVIDIA driver on your system is too old (found version 12080). Please update your GPU driver by downloading and installing a new version from the URL: http://www.nvidia.com/Download/index.aspx Alternatively, go to: https://pytorch.org to install a PyTorch version that has been compiled with your version of the CUDA driver.
```

The driver version is 12080 (CUDA 12.8) but PyTorch's CUDA runtime was compiled with CUDA 12.4 and requires driver 12040+.

For vLLM, since it uses spawn-based multiprocessing, each subprocess tries to init CUDA and fails because the subprocess doesn't inherit the parent's working CUDA state.


The main process loads fine because it's already initialized CUDA before spawning. I'm weighing several approaches: downgrading PyTorch to match the older CUDA driver, using torch.compile in lazy mode to defer CUDA initialization, or patching the driver version check. The simplest path forward is just running the evaluation on CPU with device_map='cpu', even though it'll be slower. Let me try forcing `VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD=spawn` and testing whether the HF model can work with CUDA despite the driver mismatch.

Actually, I realize the HF+accelerate path won't help since it also fails when `torch.cuda.init()` is called in a spawned subprocess. For lm_eval with the HF model, it uses fork, so the main process initializes CUDA once and the child processes inherit the state. The problem is that when `accelerate.dispatch_model` loads shards across devices and then lm_eval's HF model wrapper calls `self._model.to(self.device)` where `self.device` is CUDA, it fails because CUDA isn't properly initialized in that context.

The real issue is that even with `device_map='auto'`, the model might end up partially on CPU and partially on CUDA, and when HF's code calls `self.model.to(self.device)` (where self.device is "cuda:0"), it triggers a CUDA init in a context where it shouldn't. So the solution is either to use `accelerate("cuda:0")` to ensure the model stays fully on CUDA, or patch HF to avoid calling `.to()` when the model is already dispatched. Let me just try running with `--device cpu` and `device_map='cpu'` to see if that sidesteps the entire problem.




The root cause is clear now. The GPU driver (CUDA 12.8, driver version 12080) is too old for PyTorch's CUDA runtime (compiled with CUDA 12.4, requires driver 12040+). Both vLLM and HF evaluation fail on CUDA.

The only viable path is CPU evaluation. Let me run it:


**Tool call:** `write`

```json
{
  "content": "#!/bin/bash\nset -e\n\nMODEL_PATH=\"/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16\"\nOUTPUT_PATH=\"/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/lm_eval_results\"\nVENV_PY=\"/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/venv/bin/python\"\n\nTASKS=\"piqa,mmlu,hellaswag,gsm8k\"\nBATCH_SIZE=1\n\nexport PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1\n\n# CPU evaluation: GPU driver (12.8) is too old for PyTorch CUDA runtime (12.4).\n# device_map='cpu' keeps all model weights on CPU.\n\"$VENV_PY\" -m lm_eval \\\n    --model hf \\\n    --model_args \"pretrained=$MODEL_PATH,dtype=bfloat16,device_map=cpu,trust_remote_code=True\" \\\n    --tasks \"$TASKS\" \\\n    --batch_size \"$BATCH_SIZE\" \\\n    --output_path \"$OUTPUT_PATH\"",
  "file_path": "/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/evaluate.sh"
```

Actually, we now know vLLM doesn't work (driver issue) and HF with CUDA doesn't work (driver issue). Only CPU works.

For a Qwen3-9B model on CPU, loglikelihood computation (no autoregressive generation needed) should be much faster than generation. Let me estimate:


- piqa: 1838 docs, each needs loglikelihood of ~50 tokens × 2 choices = ~100 forward passes
- Each forward pass: ~50 tokens, ~0.5s on CPU = 50s per doc × 1838 = ~25 hours for piqa alone

That's way too slow. But actually, loglikelihood is just a forward pass without autoregressive generation, so each forward pass processes the full context once. For piqa with context length ~50 tokens, that's much faster than I initially estimated.

If each token takes about 10ms on CPU, then 50 tokens is 0.5s per pass, or 1s per document with 2 choices. That puts piqa at roughly 30 minutes and gsm8k around 45 minutes with its longer contexts. I should start running and monitor progress with periodic polling.

The quantization used RTN (Round-to-Nearest) method and auto_round format.

**Thinking**

This is helpful! The CPU inference is ~1.62s per forward pass for ~80 tokens. So generation is very slow. But the loglikelihood computation might be faster since it doesn't need autoregressive generation.

Looking at the lm_eval output earlier, it was processing the loglikelihood phase quickly (1838 docs processed), but then got stuck on `generate_until` for gsm8k.

Let me check what "generate_until" means in lm_eval. For multiple choice tasks like piqa, the evaluation uses loglikelihood (ranking). For generation tasks like gsm8k, it uses greedy generation which is much slower.

For piqa (multiple choice), lm_eval uses loglikelihood which only needs ONE forward pass per document (to compute log probabilities of each choice). That's fast.

For gsm8k (math word problems), it needs autoregressive generation which on CPU would be extremely slow (potentially minutes per question).

Let me just run the evaluation with piqa only first to get a quick result, then try the other tasks.

Interesting! Now it says "Using device 'cuda:0'" even though we're using `device_map='cpu'`! This means `device_map='cpu'` is being treated as the `device` kwarg in HFLM.__init__, which overrides the default. But then somehow the model is still on CPU...

Wait - looking at the log more carefully:
```
2026-04-29:21:31:02 INFO     [models.huggingface:161] Using device 'cuda:0'
```

This is suspicious. The HFLM code would show `cuda:0` here if `device` was passed as an arg, but then it should crash when trying to do `self.model.to(self.device)`. But we're not seeing that crash.


So the model was loaded with `device_map='cpu'` placing it on CPU, but then the self._device got set to CUDA somehow, which would normally cause a mismatch during generation. Yet there's no crash reported, so maybe the generation is just running very slowly on CPU instead of actually failing. The CUDA warning from torch is appearing in the logs, but that shouldn't happen if the model is truly on CPU—unless there's something else going on with how the device is being resolved.

Wait, I think I'm seeing it now. When `device='cuda:0'` is passed to HFLM, it sets `self._device = cuda:0` even though the actual model tensors are on CPU due to `device_map='cpu'`. The `self.model.to(self.device)` call would then fail because it's trying to move already-placed tensors, but that's being caught and ignored. So the model stays on CPU while lm_eval thinks it's on CUDA, which is why everything appears to work but runs unbearably slow. me trace through what's actually happening here. Looking at the HFLM initialization code, `self._device` gets set to `cuda:0` from the device kwarg, but then when the model is created with `device_map='cpu'`, the actual tensors end up on CPU. The `self.model.to(self.device)` call would then try to move an already-placed model to CUDA, which should fail—but maybe the error is being silently ignored somehow? I need to check the actual huggingface.py code to see if there's an exception handler that's swallowing this error.

                            f"Truncating {total_length - self.max_length + 1} tokens from the left."

### [2026-04-29 21:35:55 UTC] ASSISTANT

**Thinking**

The `device=self.device` at line 1226 creates the tensor on self._device. The problem is the torch._C._cuda_init call happens when torch.cuda is accessed in ANY way, even just to check if CUDA is available.

The fix I need is to patch torch.cuda to never even try to access CUDA. But since it's lazy, the issue must be that something else (like the quantization config or something else in transformers) is triggering CUDA init.

Actually wait - the model IS using device_map='cpu' which means all weights are on CPU. The lm_eval HFLM correctly identifies that the model is on CPU (no GPU usage). And self._device is set to 'cuda:0' because the CLI --device cuda is passed.

So inp = torch.tensor(..., device=self.device) puts the tensor on cuda:0. Then the model does self._model(inputs) where model is on CPU and inputs are on GPU. This causes the implicit transfer.

BUT - then the real crash happens at line 1226: `torch.tensor(..., device=self.device)` - this itself triggers CUDA lazy init because torch.tensor with device=torch.device('cuda') needs CUDA to be initialized first.

The fix: Change self._device to 'cpu' even when model is on CUDA device_map. This way torch.tensor creates tensors on CPU and no CUDA init is needed.

But we can't modify lm_eval's HFLM. Or can we?

What if we set the environment variable `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` to make torch.cuda.device_count() return 0? Then self._device would be set to 'cpu' instead of 'cuda'.

Wait but the CLI --device cuda overrides self._device determination. Let me check again...

Looking at the code:


```python
if device and device in device_list:
    self._device = torch.device(device)  # Sets to 'cuda:0' because --device cuda is in device_list
```

So --device cuda overrides any automatic detection. Even if we unset CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. The CLI --device cuda explicitly sets self._device = torch.device('cuda').

But here's the thing: if I set --device cpu instead, self._device becomes torch.device('cpu') and all tensors get created on CPU. The model is on CPU too (device_map='cpu'), so there's no mismatch and no CUDA init needed.

The real problem is that torch.cuda.is_available() returns True because the CUDA runtime is installed even though the driver is too old. When lm_eval checks torch.cuda.is_available() during initialization, it triggers the lazy CUDA init which then fails because the driver is incompatible. So by setting --device cpu, I bypass the entire CUDA initialization path and everything runs on CPU where it works.

But I can't just change --device to cpu because that would prevent the GPU from being used when it actually IS available on a system with a proper driver. I need a way to detect if CUDA is truly usable before torch.cuda.is_available() gets called. Maybe by checking torch.version.cuda explicitly or setting an environment variable that torch checks before attempting initialization. If CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is set to an empty string, the NVIDIA libraries won't be loaded, causing torch.cuda.is_available() to return False and forcing torch.device to default to 'cpu'. But the --device argument from the command line gets validated against device_list and takes precedence, so even with CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES="" and --device cuda passed, self._device would still be set to 'cuda:0' since 'cuda' is in device_list.

The real problem is that torch._C._cuda_init() gets called later when creating tensors on that device. The solution is to patch lm_eval's HFLM to create tensors on the actual device where the model is located rather than wherever self._device points. For CPU-only models with device_map='cpu', tensors should land on 'cpu', not 'cuda:0'.


### [2026-04-29 21:36:07 UTC] ASSISTANT

**Thinking**

The process died. Let me write a better patch and restart. The issue is that even though the model is on CPU (device_map='cpu'), self._device is 'cuda:0' because torch.cuda.is_available() returns True (the CUDA device exists even though it's too old to use). And when torch.tensor(..., device=self.device) is called with self.device='cuda:0', it tries to initialize CUDA and fails.

I need to patch the HFLM class to:
1. Set self._device to CPU when device_map='cpu' is specified in model_args
2. OR patch the torch.tensor() calls to use a device that works


3. OR prevent torch.cuda.is_available() from returning True in the first place by setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""

Actually, unsetting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is the cleanest approach—when there are no visible CUDA devices, torch.cuda.is_available() returns False and self._device defaults to CPU instead. I should update evaluate.sh to include this.


**Tool call:** `write`

```json
{
  "content": "#!/bin/bash\nset -e\n\nMODEL_PATH=\"/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16\"\nOUTPUT_PATH=\"/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/lm_eval_results\"\nVENV_PY=\"/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/venv/bin/python\"\n\nTASKS=\"piqa,mmlu,hellaswag,gsm8k\"\nBATCH_SIZE=1\n\nexport PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1\n# CRITICAL: Hide CUDA devices. The system's old NVIDIA driver (12.8) is incompatible\n# with PyTorch's CUDA 12.4 runtime. torch.cuda.is_available() returns True but\n# any actual CUDA operation (tensor creation, initialization) fails.\n# By unsetting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, we make CUDA unavailable so lm_eval\n# falls back to CPU (self._device = cpu), and device_map='cpu' places model on CPU.\nunset CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES\n\n\"$VENV_PY\" -m lm_eval \\\n    --model hf \\\n    --model_args \"pretrained=$MODEL_PATH,dtype=bfloat16,device_map=cpu,trust_remote_code=True\" \\\n    --tasks \"$TASKS\" \\\n    --batch_size \"$BATCH_SIZE\" \\\n    --output_path \"$OUTPUT_PATH\" \\\n    --device cpu",

}
```

Actually, wait. Looking at the memory usage, lm_eval is using 17GB of RAM, which matches exactly what I'd expect for a BF16 9B model loaded on CPU (18 bytes per parameter). I think what's happening is that the 2077 it/s is just measuring context construction, not actual model inference. The loglikelihood computation probably isn't running through the model at all during that phase—it's just building contexts. The real model inference only happens during the generate_until phase, which is what's taking 363 seconds per item on CPU. That would explain why piqa looks so fast in the logs but gsm8k is crawling.

Looking at the CPU usage more carefully, 512% suggests multiple cores are being used for inference, but each item is still taking 6 minutes because the model forward pass is fundamentally slow on CPU. The math doesn't quite add up—if the model can do 2077 items per second, that should translate to much faster generation than 363 seconds per item, so something about how the timing is being measured or what's actually being benchmarked doesn't align.

I'm realizing the tokenizer might be the bottleneck during context construction, which would explain the fast tokenization speed, but the actual model forward pass for generation should still be much quicker than what's being observed. With a 9B parameter model and 187GB RAM available, loading the full model on CPU is feasible, and while generation throughput would be limited, it shouldn't take 363 seconds per item—that's unusually slow for token generation even on CPU.

The piqa loglikelihood computation completing in under a second suggests the model is already loaded and cached, so the delay during generate_until isn't from model loading. The most probable explanation is that generate_until isn't running on GPU due to the CUDA visibility issue, forcing CPU inference, which would be expected to be slower than GPU generation. If CPU inference is indeed what's happening, then the real question is whether the current task configuration—running on CPU with explicit device settings—is actually what was intended.

The evaluation can continue as-is since it's processing correctly, though slowly at ~363s/item for GSM8K. Alternatively, I could try GPU execution with the cuda device specified while unsetting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES to prevent device mapping from auto-detecting and overriding the setting, forcing the model to stay on GPU despite the device_map configuration. I'm realizing the real issue: when lm_eval loads with `device=cuda`, it tries to create tensors on CUDA even though the model itself is on CPU with device_map. - With 43844 total items, that's roughly 50+ hours total, which confirms my calculation.

Given the infeasibility of CPU evaluation and the CUDA initialization errors with GPU, I need to explore alternative approaches. Let me try a different strategy: if I can patch PyTorch's CUDA initialization to not lazily call `torch.cuda.init()`, I might be able to use the GPU after all, since the actual CUDA operations (tensor creation and computations) should work fine once initialization is complete.

But I'm also wondering if vLLM with different worker configurations might offer a faster path. Actually, let me reconsider the numbers - I couldn't get the vLLM model to load because it was stuck waiting for GPU during initialization. So vLLM is out too.

Let me try something different: I'll patch PyTorch's CUDA to always return False for `is_available()` by setting an environment variable. This way, lm_eval won't even attempt CUDA operations and will fall back to CPU-only mode from the start.

Actually, the real issue is that while `torch.cuda.is_available()` returns True due to the lazy initialization, the actual CUDA operations fail with the "CUDA driver version" error. I could patch the `CUDA_init` function to silently succeed without actually initializing anything, or I could use environment variables to disable CUDA entirely. Setting `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=-1` or `PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF` with proper settings might prevent the lazy initialization from causing problems.

But wait - the model was already partially loaded on the GPU during the CPU evaluation attempt. I saw "CUDA initialization: found version 12080" in the logs before it switched to CPU mode. Let me examine the actual process output to understand where exactly the CUDA operations were failing.

The key insight is that `torch.cuda.is_available()` returns True (lazy init succeeds), but actual tensor creation fails. I need to set `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` before PyTorch is imported to make `torch.cuda.device_count() == 0` and force CPU fallback. Alternatively, patching `_cuda_init` to catch the driver error might work. Let me try the environment variable approach first - setting `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` in the bash script before running the Python command should prevent the GPU from being visible to PyTorch entirely.

### [2026-04-29 22:09:46 UTC] ASSISTANT

**Thinking**

Memory usage is growing: 40GB used, 6GB free. The system has 187GB total, so it's using about 40GB now. 

The model is 9B parameters in BF16 = ~18GB. The model is being loaded with `device_map=auto` and `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""`. Since CUDA is not visible, `device_map=auto` should fall back to CPU... but we're seeing 40GB of RAM usage.

Wait - maybe `device_map=auto` actually DOES load the model to CPU when GPU is unavailable! That would explain the high memory usage. The model weights (18GB for BF16) + KV cache + activations for generation would explain the 40GB.


But I'm confused about how the model is even functioning on CPU if the earlier test with `torch.cuda.is_available()` returned False. Let me trace through what's happening with CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES being set to empty.

When I unset it completely, CUDA becomes visible again, so torch.cuda.is_available() returns True. In that scenario, `device_map=auto` tries to load the model onto GPU first. But we've already seen that attempting CUDA operations causes a crash—likely because the model parameters get placed on GPU initially but then fail when actually used.

The real issue is that loading with `device_map=auto` puts tensors on GPU, and when lm_eval later tries to move inputs to `self._device` (which becomes CUDA), it's already too late. The CUDA operations themselves trigger the driver incompatibility error.

If I explicitly set `device_map='cpu'` instead, that forces all model tensors directly to CPU from the start, avoiding the GPU placement altogether. But in my current run with `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` and `device_map=auto`, the 40GB memory footprint suggests the model might actually be loading onto GPU after all, which would eventually cause the same failure. Let me check the actual GPU memory usage to see what's really happening.

```json
}
```

Let me take a step back. I have a very specific situation:

1. The system's NVIDIA driver is version 12.8, which is incompatible with PyTorch's CUDA 12.4 runtime
2. torch.cuda.is_available() returns True (lazy init succeeds), but any actual CUDA tensor operation fails
3. CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES="" makes device_count=0, but then self._device = cpu, which requires device_map='cpu' to work

```json
  "command": "cat > /tmp/patch_cuda_detection.py << 'PYEOF'\n# Patch torch.cuda.is_available to return False when the driver is incompatible\nimport torch\n\n# Save original\noriginal_is_available = torch.cuda.is_available\n\n# Check if CUDA actually works\ntry:\n    test_tensor = torch.zeros(10, device='cuda')\n    cuda_works = True\nexcept Exception as e:\n    cuda_works = False\n    print(f\"CUDA detection: is_available={torch.cuda.is_available()}, but actual ops fail: {e}\")\n\nif not cuda_works:\n    # Monkey-patch torch.cuda.is_available to return False\n    torch.cuda.is_available = lambda: False\n    print(\"Patched torch.cuda.is_available() to return False\")\n    print(\"Now CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES='' will properly hide GPU and device_map='auto' will use CPU\")\nPYEOF\n/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/venv/bin/python /tmp/patch_cuda_detection.py 2>&1",
```

### [2026-04-29 22:30:09 UTC] ASSISTANT

**Thinking**

This proves the fix: monkey-patching `torch.cuda.is_available` to return `False` when the actual CUDA ops fail forces the code to properly handle the "no GPU" case and fall back to CPU correctly.

The real fix is to set `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` which makes `device_count=0` regardless of what `is_available` returns. But in the forked subprocess, `torch.cuda.is_available()` returns `True` (lazy init succeeds), and `device_count=1` (visible device 0).

However, the key issue is that when `device_map='auto'` is used, `accelerate` does its own device assignment based on `device_count`. With `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""`, `device_count=0` so `accelerate` falls back to CPU. The problem was that the forked subprocess's `torch.cuda.is_available()` returned `True` but actual ops failed.


The solution is `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` so that `device_count=0` makes accelerate assign to CPU, and `self._device` becomes `cpu` so tensors are created on CPU. The monkey-patch proves the concept but isn't the final solution—`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""` is. I should verify that the log contains the necessary output to proceed with this approach.

gpus = torch.cuda.device_count() = 0, so gpus >= 1 is False. 

Then self._device gets set to cpu. But then self._model._device is set by from_pretrained when device_map='auto', and since there are no visible GPUs, accelerate places the model on CPU. So self._device = cpu while self._model.device = cpu as well—they should be consistent.

The issue might be that when device='cuda' is passed to from_pretrained with no GPUs visible, it could fail. But actually, the device argument is just a default that device_map overrides, so it should work fine. Or maybe the real problem is that self.model is a wrapped object in HFAccelerateHFLM, and self.model.to(self.device) gets called somewhere, which could be problematic if self.model is already on CPU. When the model is already on CPU and you call `.to(cpu)`, PyTorch doesn't error—it's a no-op. So that's probably not it either. Looking at the HFLM code, the model is an AutoModelForCausalLM with device_map='auto' set during from_pretrained, so it should already be on the correct device and calling `.to()` again shouldn't cause issues.

But wait, let me trace through the loglikelihood method more carefully. The `context_enc` and `attn_masks` are tensors being moved to `self.device` (cpu), and then the model (also on cpu) processes them. That should work fine on CPU.

The real problem might be that the actual CUDA operations are failing somewhere deeper in the model computation, not in the data movement. When self._device is cpu but the model is also cpu, context_enc.to(cpu) should work. Unless the issue is with torch.compile or some other backend that's trying to use CUDA when it shouldn't.

But actually, the model is already dispatched across devices via device_map='auto', so calling .to(cpu) again shouldn't cause issues. The no_kernel_image error typically happens when the kernel wasn't compiled for that GPU architecture—like using a kernel built for compute capability 8.6 on a 7.5 device. The issue is likely that `torch.cuda.is_available()` returns True (allowing device_map='auto' to attempt GPU placement), but the actual GPU operations fail because the CUDA version doesn't support the GPU architecture.

The problem is that lm_eval's self._device is determined by `gpus = torch.cuda.device_count()` which returns 1 since GPU is visible, so self._device gets set to "cuda:0" even though the GPU can't actually run the kernels. Meanwhile, the model might end up on CPU (since device_map='auto' might fall back due to kernel errors) or remain on GPU with broken kernels. This mismatch between self._device (cuda) and actual tensor placement could cause issues. A potential fix would be to patch `torch.cuda.is_available` to return False so that device_map='auto' doesn't attempt GPU placement in the first place.

Let me try a completely different approach. Instead of running lm_eval directly, let me write a Python wrapper that:
1. Patches torch.cuda.is_available to return False before importing transformers/lm_eval
2. Uses CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES="" to ensure device_count=0
3. Loads model on CPU with device_map='cpu'
4. Runs evaluation using simple_evaluate from lm_eval

This should bypass the fork issue because I won't be relying on the CLI subprocess mechanism.


I'm also thinking the timing might be the culprit here - the nohup script runs immediately but the Python process hangs before producing output. I should try running lm_eval directly with just piqa to see if that makes a difference.


**Tool call:** `write`

```json
{
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"\nDirect evaluation script that patches CUDA detection before loading anything.\nThis bypasses the fork issue by patching torch.cuda.is_available before\nimporting transformers/lm_eval.\n\"\"\"\nimport sys\nimport os\n\n# CRITICAL: Patch CUDA detection BEFORE importing torch/transformers/lm_eval\n# The system's NVIDIA driver (12.8) is too old for PyTorch CUDA 12.4 runtime.\n# torch.cuda.is_available() returns True (lazy init succeeds) but actual tensor\n# operations fail. We patch it to return False so accelerate uses CPU.\nimport torch\n_orig_is_available = torch.cuda.is_available\n\ndef _patched_is_available():\n    result = _orig_is_available()\n    if result:\n        try:\n            # Test if CUDA actually works\n            t = torch.zeros(1, device='cuda')\n            del t\n            torch.cuda.synchronize()\n        except Exception:\n            # CUDA detection lies - patch it\n            result = False\n    return False  # Always return False to force CPU\n\ntorch.cuda.is_available = _patched_is_available\nprint(\"Patched torch.cuda.is_available()\", file=sys.stderr)\n\n# Now imports will see the patched version\nimport torch  # noqa\nfrom lm_eval.simple_evaluate import simple_evaluate\n\nMODEL_PATH = '/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16'\nOUTPUT_PATH = '/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/lm_eval_results'\nTASKS = ['piqa', 'hellaswag', 'mmlu', 'gsm8k']\n\nprint(\"Starting evaluation...\", file=sys.stderr)\nprint(f\"Model: {MODEL_PATH}\", file=sys.stderr)\nprint(f\"Tasks: {TASKS}\", file=sys.stderr)\nprint(f\"torch.cuda.is_available(): {torch.cuda.is_available()}\", file=sys.stderr)\n\n# Use CPU device_map since we're patching CUDA away\nmodel_args = f\"pretrained={MODEL_PATH},dtype=bfloat16,device_map=cpu,trust_remote_code=True\"\n\nresults = simple_evaluate(\n    model='hf',\n    model_args=model_args,\n    tasks=TASKS,\n    batch_size='auto',\n    device='cpu',\n    output_path=OUTPUT_PATH,\n)\n\nprint(\"Evaluation complete!\", file=sys.stderr)\n\n# Save results\nimport json\nresult_file = os.path.join(OUTPUT_PATH, 'results.json')\nwith open(result_file, 'w') as f:\n    json.dump(results, f, indent=2)\n\nprint(f\"Results saved to: {result_file}\", file=sys.stderr)",
  "file_path": "/root/.openclaw/workspace/quantized/runs/Jackrong_Qwopus3.5-9B-v3-W4A16/direct_eval.py"
}
```
